briefing.text: Alright, listen up. Villa on the hill, three floors, east wing is where the money lives. Owner's at the opera. We've got two hours before the car comes back.
Beautiful property. Shame about the security system. Villa layout, two stairwells — use the service entrance, it's on a different circuit.
The villa's got cameras on the garden-facing side. Everywhere else, they went cheap. Typical.
Owner thinks a gate and a night guard is enough. The villa's theirs; the take is ours. Move when I say move.
Tonight the mark is {mark}. {vault} is where we're headed. Security: {security}. They're sitting on {targetHaul} and they don't know we're coming.
{mark}. Owner's not home. {vault} is where the money is. Security's rated {security} — I've worked worse.
Big estate. Forty rooms. We only care about six of them — and I've got the layout. Staff quarters are in the west annex, well away from us.
This estate has been in the family three generations. Three generations of hiding things they shouldn't have. We're just collecting.
Groundskeepers are off on Thursdays. It's Thursday. That's not luck — I made it Thursday. Welcome to the estate.
The estate's old money. Old money means old security. I've seen worse. I've also seen better — don't get cocky.
The mark is {mark}. {targetHaul} sitting in {vault}, behind {security} security. Three generations of wealth, one generation of poor decisions. Let's be that decision.
{mark} — old money, older locks. {vault} is our destination. Security grade {security}. {targetHaul} if we do this right. And we do this right.
Forty-second floor. Views are incredible, I'm told. Security's private, not police — that matters when things get loud.
Penthouse suite, two units merged into one. The owner knocked out the wall between them. Thoughtful of him — more room for the collection.
Service lift bypasses the lobby desk. That's how we get up. How we get out, well, that's what I'm paying you for.
Penthouse. Private elevator, one stairwell, and a building manager who takes long lunches. Today's the day.
We're going into {mark}. {vault}, {security} security. The owner's spent {targetHaul} on art alone — that's just what we can see from the lobby. Let's find out what's behind the doors.
{mark}. {targetHaul} in play. Security: {security}. {vault} is the target. Any questions? No. Good. Let's go.

obstacleClue.text: That safe's a Hartmann 1400 — three tumblers, left-right-left sequence. Listen for the clicks. Third one's loud, you'll know it.
The safe reads as a standard combination lock. It's not. Those tumblers are magnetic-assisted — turn slow, let them seat.
Someone put a good safe in a bad spot. The vibration from the HVAC throws the tumblers — work between the cycle pulses.
Tumblers, combination, the usual. Except the last digit resets if you overshoot. Get it right the first time.
I've cracked that model twice before. The sweet spot on the third tumbler is exactly two marks past center. Trust me.
Three-tumbler safe. The mechanism's aged — it over-rotates on the second pass. Compensate by going slow on the return.
Electronic lock with a rhythm pad. Sixteen beats, enter the right pattern. I've got the pattern — memorize it before you touch it.
Audio-keyed entry system. Sounds fancy, it's not — same sequence every time, just needs steady timing. Don't rush it.
The rhythm lock is temperamental. Too fast and it rejects you; too slow and the timeout kicks in. Find the tempo and hold it.
Beat sequence lock — the kind they sell to people who think they're being clever. The pattern's on the inside of the lid, so I'm told. Ignore the lid, I've got the pattern.
Sixteen-beat entry. The counter resets on a wrong note, not the whole sequence — recover fast if you slip.
Rhythm pad lock. The key is consistency — the system's checking timing as much as sequence. Play it like you mean it.
Biometric bypass. The panel runs a knowledge check — categorisation, basically. Answer right and it thinks you're authorized. It's a stupid system.
Security question gate. The owner personalised it — answers are somewhere in this room. Look fast.
The system challenges you with a category sort. It's testing that you belong here. You don't — but answer like you do.
Category challenge lock. Six correct classifications opens it. One wrong answer and the timer drops by half. Pace yourself.
Old-school knowledge gate — someone thought this was more secure than a PIN. Wrong things, right category, and it opens right up.
Category sort panel. The system's looking for pattern recognition. Sort fast and sort right — it counts every correct group.
Room's been staged. Owner hid something and made it look natural. Find what doesn't fit — something's off in here and it's not the decor.
The paintings are real. The books are real. One item in this room is a prop — and the real item is behind it. Quick eyes, quick hands.
Lot of valuables in here. Too many to check them all. Look for what's slightly wrong — a seam, a shadow, something that doesn't sit right.
The once-over, people. Scan the room fast, find the fake, move on. We didn't pay for the full tour.
Interior designer confirmed the room was done in two phases. Phase two is the cover — what's underneath phase one is what we want.
The object we're after is in plain sight. It's just disguised as something ordinary. Don't overthink it — your first instinct is usually right.
Electrical bypass. The circuit runs through four relay panels — kill them in sequence or the alarm fires. Sequence matters.
That's a redundant loop circuit. You can't just cut one wire — you follow the path and disable each node. Here, I'm sending you the schematic.
The alarm system runs on a ring topology. Break it anywhere without following the loop and every panel lights up. Go in order.
Old wiring, new alarm system. Whoever installed this cut corners — the trace is messier than it should be. Follow the wire, not the diagram.
Circuit bypass. The nodes pulse in a pattern — hit each one on its off-cycle. Miss the rhythm and the current spikes.
Four nodes, one sequence. The circuit is self-monitoring — touching any node out of order resets the whole thing. Start at the panel behind the cabinet.
Trivia lock. Owner's an enthusiast — answers are domain-specific. I've done the reading so you don't have to. Trust the earpiece.
Security system runs a knowledge gate — three right answers, you're in. These people think obscurity equals security. It doesn't.
The lock challenges you on the owner's interests. Art, wine, vintage cars — pick one and go deep. Or just listen to me.
Inside knowledge bypass. The owner set the questions personally. That was his first mistake. His second was keeping a diary.
Trivia gate. Five questions, all related. Get four right minimum. I'll feed you the answers — just don't second-guess me.
Knowledge check panel. The owner's a collector — questions run to provenance, dates, attribution. I've studied the collection. You just need to repeat after me.
Rotary safe, four-digit code. The owner changes it quarterly and writes it somewhere in the study. One of you should be looking while the other cracks.
Wall safe, hidden behind the mirror. Standard rotary. The dial's got some wear on a few numbers — that's your shortlist.
The safe's a Diebold 550. Not the hardest model, but not the easiest either. Stethoscope approach, or feel for the rebound.
I had a copy of the combination six months ago. They changed it. But they only ever change the last two digits. Start there.
Combination safe. Four tumblers. Drop into the detents slowly — rushing will skip a setting and you'll have to start over.
The safe has a relocker. One forced attempt and it seizes. Listen for the tumbler drop on each digit — that's your confirmation before you move to the next.
Pressure sensor mesh under the display case. Step too hard or lift the piece wrong and the alarm trips. Slow and precise.
Laser grid in that room, intermittent pattern. I'm tracking the cycle — I'll tell you when to move. Don't move before I say.
Vibration sensor on the case. It's calibrated for impact, not contact — lift straight up, don't slide. Steady as you go.
The piece is wired directly. Disconnect sequence is documented — I've got it. The order matters more than the speed.
Micro-sensor in the pedestal. I've bypassed the main circuit, but the local sensor runs on its own battery. You move it, it clocks it. Don't move it until I say.
Weight-sensitive plinth. The sensor reads change — too fast or too slow and it triggers. Match the pace I give you, exactly.
The documents are filed in sections across three cabinets — I need all four parts. Work fast, work together, the clock's ticking.
Distributed filing system. Smart, actually — no single point of failure. Except now I need you to hit all the points. Spread out.
Evidence is partitioned deliberately — each piece is in a different location. You need all of them for this to mean anything. Move together.
The item's split up. Someone was paranoid. It's one room, five stations — assemble the whole thing before any single part sets off the sensor.
The target isn't a single object. It's a set. Each piece is loose somewhere in the room. Coordinate — call out what you find.
Multi-part target. The owner split it to prevent exactly this — five components, one room. You reassemble on the move. Don't stop until you've got all of them.
Silent alarm system, multi-zone. Cutting one zone trips the others. There's a disarm sequence — the sequence is what matters.
Someone spent money on this alarm system. The panel is behind the painting, has eight zones, and a dead-man circuit. You have to disarm in order.
The alarm is set to the silent type — won't make a sound in the room, but dispatches a response team. You've got maybe four minutes once it trips.
The wiring's colour-coded but the colours lie. The installer was cheap, used what he had. Red is not red. Go by position, not colour.
Multi-stage alarm. Four stages, each needs a different input. I'll walk you through it — but if you're not listening, we're both going to have a bad night.
Six-zone alarm panel, dead-man circuit on zones three and five. The sequence is specific and it's timed. I'll count you through — don't skip ahead.

optionDescription.text: Play it by the book. Slower, quieter, less heat.
The careful approach. We take what we came for and leave clean.
Conservative play. Lower risk, lower take — but we walk out.
Standard approach. No unnecessary noise, no unnecessary heat.
Low-profile. We do the job, we disappear.
The quiet option. Less loot, but the job stays clean.
Push harder. More on the table — more heat if it goes wrong.
Double down. The payout's there, and so is the exposure.
High-risk, high-reward. Somebody's going to have a very bad day.
The greedy option. Every run has one — question is whether this is the run to take it.
More loot, more heat. That's the trade. Make the call.
Reach for it. More upside, more exposure. Eyes open.
Controlled. Take the certain win and leave the bonus on the table.
The textbook option. Nothing flashy — just clean execution.
Standard play. We came here for the job, not the jackpot.
Safe and steady. Loot secured, heat stays manageable.
The aggressive option. Big take, big exposure. Worth it — if you're good enough.
Push your luck. The upside is real and so is the cost of failure.
All in on the big take. Just know what you're signing up for.
The greedy call. Higher loot, higher heat — always has been.

scenarioSetup.text: Hold on — something's changed in here. I'm seeing it on the feed. Listen to what I'm about to tell you.
Okay, slight complication. Not a crisis, just a situation. Here's the situation.
Reading you a scenario. This one's live — I'm seeing it happen right now on the cameras.
Something just landed in your lap. This is the part where you make a call.
The job just got interesting. Pay attention — this is what I need you to deal with.
Heads up. New information from my end. It affects your next move, so let me finish before you react.
Van to crew: you've got a development. Not necessarily bad — depends what you do with it.
Pause. I need thirty seconds of your attention before you take another step.
Stand by. Something just changed in this picture and I need you to hear it before you move.
Crew, this is the van. I've got a situation developing on the feed. Here's what I'm seeing.
This job just handed you a variable. Here's the variable — and here's what I need from you.
Hold position. New information, relevant to what you're about to do. I'll be quick.

outcomeQuip.text: Textbook. That's what I like to see. Move on.
Clean. Not a scratch on it. You made that look easy.
Zero heat. I could kiss you. Don't stop.
Professional. That's the word I'd use. Also, move.
Clean exit on that one. The meter didn't even flinch.
No fuss, no trail. I trained you well. Next room.
That's what surgical looks like. No alarm, no trace — don't get comfortable.
Invisible. They'll be checking this room for a week and finding nothing. Keep the pace.
You got through it. Messy, but through it. Costs us a little heat.
Bit rough around the edges — the system noticed something. We'll manage it.
That was... fine. Not good. Fine. The heat's up but we're not blown.
One step forward, half a step back. The heat ticked up, but we're still in it.
They noticed something but couldn't place it. That's the good news. The bad news is the meter's climbing.
Minor alarm response. They're checking, not chasing. Keep moving — we've still got the advantage.
Heat's ticked but the job's not done. Sloppy costs us — file that away for next time.
Security's curious, not certain. That's the difference between a complication and a catastrophe. Use it.
That was bad. Not a disaster, but bad. The heat just spiked and now we run a tighter ship.
Completely botched. I'm not angry — okay I'm a little angry. The meter's hot, we need to compensate.
They know someone's in here. They don't know where yet. Move like they do.
Heat's at uncomfortable levels. That one cost us real ground. Make the next one count.
Security's now actively investigating. That's what botched gets you. Keep your heads down.
That noise will have reached somebody's ears. We're on borrowed time now — don't waste it.
Messy. Very messy. They know something's wrong — they just don't know what yet. Move.
That one hurt us. We're still operational, but only just. Don't give them another opening.

pushRun.text: Good haul so far. We can push deeper and take more, or I can bring the car around right now.
You've got the loot. Question is, do you want more of it? I'm watching the clock.
Take stock. You've done well. The smart play might be the exit — or the brave play might be one more room.
We're drawing attention. This is the moment. Cut your losses or push your luck — I need an answer now.
The heat's getting real. I'm pulling the car to the south exit. Say the word and we're gone — or say you want more and I'll give you two minutes.
Alright, moment of truth. Do we roll with what we've got, or do you want another crack at the prize room?
Everything's still manageable. That can change fast. Make the call.
I can hear dispatch on the scanner. They're not here yet but they will be. This is the escape window — it won't stay open.
The haul is secured and the exit's clean. The question is whether you're satisfied.
The job says take what you have. Your instincts say there's more. Both are probably right — you decide.
Heat's real but manageable. There are rooms you haven't touched. Whether that's an opportunity depends on what you call next.
One more room is a risk. Walking now is a choice. Either way it's yours to make — I'll back it.

getawayIntro.text: Alright, it's go time. I've got the car two blocks east — meet me and do not stop walking.
Getaway clock is running. Every second in the building now is a second closer to a patrol car.
We're in the getaway phase, people. I'll handle the route — you handle the speed.
Time to earn the second half of your cut. Exit route's live, car is warm, let's go.
You made it to the run. Don't celebrate yet — we're not clean until the building's behind us.
Extraction in progress. I need you moving. No goodbyes, no souvenirs.
Getaway is live. Two blocks north, my car. Walk — don't run — until you're around the corner.
The job is done. Now do the harder part: leave cleanly. Car's at the rendezvous. Move.
Exit protocol in effect. {mark} is behind you. Keep it that way — get to the car.
You're in the getaway window. It's finite and it's closing. I need all of you moving now.

getawayCountdown.text: Keep going. The car's right there. Don't look back.
Thirty seconds. If anything's going wrong, I don't want to know until we're driving.
Move, move, move. This is why we practised the exit route.
Nearly clear. Eyes forward. Almost done.
Final stretch. I can see you on the cameras — you're almost there.
The gap is closing. You want the car to wait, you keep moving.
Almost there. Security's still running the wrong corridor — use it.
You're doing well. Don't celebrate until the car door closes behind you.
Thirty metres. The city doesn't know you yet. Keep it that way.
This is the part you don't rush. Steady, steady, go.

winSting.text: That's a clean score. The kind you tell stories about — not tonight, but eventually.
We're out. Clean. The take is real and the heat is behind us. Beautiful job.
In the clear. Count the money later — right now just appreciate that you pulled it off.
Everybody made it. The loot's in the bag. That's the job done.
Clean getaway. You might actually be as good as I thought you were.
That's how it's done. Now disappear for a while and let the heat settle.
Clean exit, good haul. {mark} won't know exactly what hit them until they check the accounts.
That's the job done right. Everyone home, everything paid. File tonight under 'professional'.
Good haul and clean air all the way out. That is what we came here to do.
Out clean. If anyone asks where you were tonight, you were somewhere boring. Practice the answer.

bustSting.text: The heat caught us. The haul's reduced — but the crew's intact. We regroup, we learn, we do better.
That's a bust. Still alive, still free — for now. Some days that's the best outcome available.
We got sloppy, or we got unlucky — doesn't matter which, the result's the same. Take the lesson.
Heat overrun. The score's reduced. I've had worse nights. Not many, but a few.
They got enough of the take. You got out. In this business, that's a qualified success.
Not the ending we planned. Still beats the alternative. Walk it off and come back sharper.
That's a bust. The take's dented but the crew's intact. The van's waiting — get in.
Not the number we wanted. Still — alive, mobile, and wiser. Regroup and file this away.
{mark} didn't yield what it promised. Some of it came home. Some didn't. That's the business.
Partial score. No casualties. The van's waiting and so is a better night — eventually.

roomApproach.text: Room {roomNum}. Stay tight, stay quiet. {mark} has no idea we're here.
Next room — {crew} stacks up. This is what we trained for.
Moving to room {roomNum}. {mark}, {vault} — that's the prize. Keep your heads.
Here we go. {crew}, on my signal. {mark} doesn't know what's coming.
Room {roomNum}. The {lane} approach is what gets us through this one.
Stack up. Room {roomNum} — inside is what we came to {mark} for. Focus.
Approaching room {roomNum}. This is {mark} territory — everything in here is leverage.
{crew} — room {roomNum}. Quiet in, quiet out. I'm watching the corridor.
Van to crew: room {roomNum}. Camera coverage is on the left side. Go right.
This is it — room {roomNum}. {mark} spent a lot on this space. Don't let that slow you down.
Room {roomNum}. I've got eyes on the corridor. You're clear for the next thirty seconds — use them.
Entering room {roomNum}. {crew} — I need you focused, not creative.
Moving on {mark}'s room {roomNum}. This is where tonight's rate gets earned.
Room {roomNum}. One at a time through the door. Discipline is what we need in here — nothing else.

scenarioReveal.text: That's the call. {mark} is none the wiser — or very, very wiser. Either way, move.
{crew} made the call. Room {roomNum} is behind us.
Decision made, consequences landing. Keep moving.
Scenario closed. {attempter} took the heat on that one — noted.
The {mark} job has one less complication. On to the next.
That's how it plays at {mark}. Van's watching — keep the pace.
The call's been made — own it and move on.
Scenario closed. Not every move looks clean from the van.
Decision done. {mark} may have noticed — or they haven't. We'll find out.
The trigger's been pulled. Keep moving.
The verdict's in. The van's watching and so is the clock.
The situation at {mark} is resolved. What matters is the next room.
Situation resolved. Good or bad, it's done. Back to work.
The verdict is in. {mark} didn't plan for this crew. Keep proving that right.
